next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

He waited three days before going back.

This was not strategy or discipline. The bruises from Carrow's detention still ached through his kneecaps whenever he climbed stairs, and the seventh floor was a long way up from the Hufflepuff basement. His left side was tender where a stinging hex had caught him across the ribs — whether Carrow had aimed there or was just a bad shot, Gavin hadn't decided. Either way, stairs were a problem.

On the third evening he went, because the ache had settled from sharp to dull and because he had not been able to stop thinking about the catalogue entry. _Sub-Collection C. Pre-Foundation Acquisitions._ A shelf number in handwriting so old the ink had gone the color of dust, and a title in a language he didn't recognize. The ink, at least, had stayed still.

He left after dinner. Miren was helping a second-year with a Switching Spell at one of the common room tables, her patience visibly thinning as the boy turned a teacup into a slightly different teacup for the fourth time running. Duncan had gone upstairs early, which he did most nights now — not because he was tired, Gavin thought, but because the common room required a version of himself that was getting harder to maintain. Nobody asked where Gavin was going. Nobody was watching him. He was the kind of person people's eyes moved past without stopping, and that had never bothered him, and tonight it was useful.

The seventh floor was quiet. An hour before curfew, but students avoided the upper corridors after dark this year — too many empty classrooms where Filch could find you...
open_hooks_to_carry: Madam Pince's hostility — she is paranoid, not helpful, and her fear of the Carrows is palpable
The detention with Amycus Carrow must be rendered with physical specificity: the stone floor under his knees, the stinging hexes, the petty pleasure Carrow takes in the punishment
prev_tail_excerpt:
...out the footnote. Pre-wand magical substrate. A manuscript in the Hogwarts collection, uncatalogued, in a part of the library system he'd never seen referenced anywhere else. Bagshot had looked at it and decided it was nothing — uncertain provenance, doubtful reliability, a notation system she couldn't read. She'd written her footnote and moved on and nobody, as far as Gavin could tell, had followed the thread.

It was probably nothing. It was almost certainly nothing. Bagshot was a real historian and she'd dismissed it and that should have been the end of it. But the thread was there now, loose and visible, the way a loose thread on a jumper was there once your finger had caught on it — not because you'd been looking for it but because it had been there all along and now you knew about it and it would still be there tomorrow, waiting to be pulled or left alone.

He'd probably look into it. Not urgently. Not with any particular expectation. Just the way you opened a drawer you'd never opened before — not expecting anything, just wanting to know what was inside.

He rolled over. The pillow was cool on the other side. From the next bed he could hear Ernie Macmillan breathing in the slow, controlled way that meant Ernie was awake and pretending not to be, and from somewhere deeper in the castle the pipes clanked once and the building shifted around them, settling into the night the way something old and heavy settled when it was finally still.

Gavin closed his eyes. The footnote sat in his bag in the dark, on a dog-eared page, in small type, waiting for nothing in particular.
state_deltas_required: The Carrow regime as physical obstacle — Gavin's search for the manuscript runs directly into the regime's surveillance and punishment apparatus
Gavin locates the manuscript's location in a sub-collection that does not appear on maps, but pays his first physical cost: detention with Amycus Carrow involving stinging hexes and forced kneeling
